what: Korok Seeds are the reward for solving 900 little hidden puzzles across Hyrule. You trade them to Hestu, a big maraca-shaped Korok, who uses them to permanently expand your three gear pouches: weapon slots, bow slots, and shield slots. More slots means you carry more gear and break fewer weapons mid-fight, so growing your inventory is one of the most useful early grinds.
hestu: Hestu moves, then settles. (1) First meeting: on the road between Dueling Peaks Stable and Kakariko Village, just past Kakariko Bridge — do the favor in the side quest The Priceless Maracas (recover his maracas from nearby Bokoblins) and he starts upgrading. (2) Next: near Riverside Stable, west of Kakariko in Hyrule Field, by a tree along the road. (3) Permanent home: Korok Forest, deep in the Great Hyrule Forest up north (Lost Woods), by the Great Deku Tree. Rising cost curve per slot — Weapons (11 upgrades): 1,2,3,5,8,12,17,25,35,45,55 = 208. Bows (8): 1,2,3,5,8,12,17,25 = 73. Shields (16): 1,2,3,4,5,10,10,10,10,10,15,15,15,15,15,20 = 160. Maxing ALL pouches costs exactly 441 seeds — that is the cap; beyond that, extra seeds do nothing for inventory. 900 seeds exist total; turning in all 900 earns Hestu's Gift, a purely cosmetic golden poop ("smells pretty bad") — a joke reward, no stats.

puzzleTypes.see: A single suspicious rock sitting by itself in an odd spot — often beside a tree, on a peak, or in a clearing where nothing else is around.
puzzleTypes.do: Walk up and pick the rock up (hold the pickup button). The Korok pops out from underneath. No tools needed — this is the most common type.

puzzleTypes.see: A small pile of leaves, a flat slab, a movable pot, or a little wall of stacked rocks that looks deliberately placed.
puzzleTypes.do: Clear or break whatever covers it — sweep the leaves, lift the slab, smash the pot, or knock the stack down — then lift the rock or grab the Korok hidden underneath.

puzzleTypes.type: Rock in a ring (drop a rock in the gap)
puzzleTypes.see: A circle of small stones with the center empty, or a ring sitting in shallow water.
puzzleTypes.do: Find a loose rock nearby and carry or throw it into the empty center to complete the circle. (In water, shoot a small rock into the middle with an arrow, or use Magnesis on a metal one.)

puzzleTypes.see: A near-symmetrical arrangement of rocks with one obviously out of place — two matching little piles where one is short a stone.
puzzleTypes.do: Pick up a nearby loose rock and place it in the gap (or rearrange the existing rocks) so the two halves match exactly.

puzzleTypes.see: A large boulder sitting near a round pit or depression in the ground.
puzzleTypes.do: Push, roll, Stasis-launch, or carry the big boulder into the pit. (Shiny metal boulders can be moved with Magnesis.)

puzzleTypes.type: Roll the boulder to the goal
puzzleTypes.see: A boulder oddly placed with no visible pit — but two trees, a marked spot, or a clear path nearby that reads as a goal.
puzzleTypes.do: Roll or push the boulder to that goal spot (often between two trees) to spawn the Korok.

puzzleTypes.see: Two cube structures meant to mirror each other, with one metal cube missing from a slot — and a loose metal cube nearby. (This is the only BotW 'cube' puzzle.)
puzzleTypes.do: Use Magnesis to grab the loose metal cube and set it into the empty slot so both structures match.

puzzleTypes.see: A metal ball — sometimes a ball-and-chain or two balls linked by a chain — sitting near a matching hole, socket, or hollow tree.
puzzleTypes.do: Use Magnesis to lift the metal ball and drop it into the hole/socket it belongs in.

puzzleTypes.type: Match the trees — remove the extra fruit
puzzleTypes.see: Two or three identical trees (cacti in the Gerudo Desert) in a row, where one has one or more extra fruit so it doesn't match its neighbors.
puzzleTypes.do: Pluck or shoot off the extra fruit so every tree has fruit in exactly the same spots.

puzzleTypes.see: A single golden/yellow Korok flower in an odd spot that vanishes and reappears a short distance away when you approach.
puzzleTypes.do: Chase it from flower to flower without missing one until it stops as a white flower — touch that final flower to spawn the Korok. (It is a chase, not a timed race.)

puzzleTypes.see: Several clusters of the same flower with different bloom counts (e.g. a clump of 1, a clump of 2, a clump of 3).
puzzleTypes.do: Touch the clusters in ascending order, fewest blooms to most. A correct touch changes its color; a wrong order resets the puzzle.

puzzleTypes.see: A tree stump marked with a leaf symbol (or a wisp/ring of light) that starts a countdown when you step on or activate it.
puzzleTypes.do: Activate it, then sprint, paraglide, or shield-surf to the goal ring before the timer runs out.

puzzleTypes.type: Shoot the lone balloon or acorn
puzzleTypes.see: A single floating balloon, or a large acorn hanging under a bridge or tucked inside a hollow tree trunk / fallen log.
puzzleTypes.do: Hit it with one arrow. (Aim down sights; Stasis can freeze a tricky one to make the shot easy.)

puzzleTypes.type: Pinwheel — shoot the targets it spawns
puzzleTypes.see: A small spinning pinwheel. Approaching it makes balloons or acorns appear — they may be moving, teleporting, or stationary.
puzzleTypes.do: Shoot down every balloon/target the pinwheel spawns before time runs out. Stasis helps pin moving ones.

puzzleTypes.type: Dive through the ring / lily-pad circle
puzzleTypes.see: A ring of light, or a circle of water lilies on a pond, sitting below a nearby ledge or cliff.
puzzleTypes.do: Climb to the ledge above and do a forward dive straight through the center of the ring.

puzzleTypes.see: A trail of glowing sparkles (Korok leaves/sparks) drifting through the air, or a lone sparkle perched at the top of a tall structure.
puzzleTypes.do: Reach and examine the sparkle — chase a moving trail to its end, or climb to the peak for a high one — to spawn the Korok.

puzzleTypes.see: A strangely shaped or out-of-place block of ice, sometimes with a sparkle frozen inside.
puzzleTypes.do: Apply fire — a torch, flame weapon, or fire arrow — to melt the ice and reveal the Korok or sparkle.

puzzleTypes.see: One or more unlit torches/braziers grouped together, often with a flame source nearby.
puzzleTypes.do: Light every torch in the set — carry fire from a flame, swing a burning weapon, or use a fire arrow until all are lit.

puzzleTypes.see: A prayer statue or a set of offering plates with one bowl empty — the other nearby bowls already hold an item (a fruit, an acorn, sometimes a rusty weapon).
puzzleTypes.do: Place an item matching the ones in the other nearby bowls into the empty bowl (e.g. an apple if the others hold apples).

hotspots: Out-of-place rocks: a single rock on a stump, peak, or cliff edge is almost always a lift-the-rock Korok — check every lonely boulder.
Circles with a gap: any ring of stones missing one piece means grab the nearest loose rock and complete it.
Tops of things: peaks, towers, ruined pillars, and lone trees on hills frequently hide rock-lift or dive-into-ring puzzles — climb up and look around.
Stables and Towers: the ground around every Stable and Sheikah Tower usually has 1-2 easy Koroks (rocks, balloons, or flower trails) right nearby.
Bright fruit/acorns on trees: an apple or acorn sitting alone on a branch or pinwheel-marked tree is a shoot-the-target Korok.
Geometry that looks 'almost right': any too-neat pile, pattern, or symmetry that's off by one piece is a complete-the-pattern puzzle.
notes: CORRECTED two arithmetic errors in the original dataset's cost totals (web-verified against ScreenRant, GamesRadar, GameWith, GoSuNoob, Zeldapedia/Fandom): the weapon-pouch total is 208 (not 203) and the shield-pouch total is 160 (not 165). The per-slot sequences themselves were correct; only the sums were off. Confirmed totals: Weapons 1,2,3,5,8,12,17,25,35,45,55 = 208; Bows 1,2,3,5,8,12,17,25 = 73; Shields 1,2,3,4,5,10,10,10,10,10,15,15,15,15,15,20 = 160. Sum 208+73+160 = 441 to max everything (the cap). Starting slots confirmed 8 weapons / 5 bows / 4 shields, ending at 19 / 13 / 20 (plus the Master Sword and Bow of Light, which sit outside the pouches). 900 seeds exist total; all-900 reward is Hestu's Gift (cosmetic golden poop, a pun on Japan's kin no unko good-luck charm). Hestu's three locations and the side quest The Priceless Maracas (recover his maracas from Bokoblins before the first upgrade) confirmed. The Korok Mask (from The Master Trials DLC) shakes to flag nearby seeds — handy for hunting beyond 441 but not needed for inventory. Puzzle-type list reflects well-documented common categories; these are community labels (the game gives no per-type official name), so phrasing describes the mechanic rather than asserting an official name. All oneLine/hint text kept under 160 chars. Confidence high on all corrected numbers.
